red.label: Missing or unreliable
criteria: Practice is absent, irregular, or manager-dependent
The team is exposed to avoidable people or delivery risk

yellow.label: Partial or inconsistent
criteria: Practice exists but is inconsistently applied
Evidence exists but is fragmented or stale
Outcomes depend too much on individual manager habits

green.label: Clear and repeatable
criteria: Practice is documented, habitual, and inspectable
Evidence is current and easy to review
Managers and employees can rely on the practice

domains.name: Legal Responsibilities
domains.description: Managers understand the basic legal and policy obligations that come with people leadership.

controls.name: Manager Legal Responsibilities
controls.description: Managers understand their legal responsibilities in the jurisdiction where they manage employees.

scoring_criteria.red: Managers have little clarity on their legal obligations and escalation paths.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Basic guidance exists, but refresh and understanding are inconsistent.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers receive and understand clear legal guidance relevant to their role and location.

domains.name: Direct Report Management
domains.description: A reliable manager cadence with each direct report covering support, feedback, goals, and performance.

controls.name: Regular 1:1 Cadence
controls.description: Managers hold weekly or biweekly 1:1s with direct reports and rarely reschedule them.

scoring_criteria.red: 1:1s are irregular, frequently canceled, or treated as optional.
scoring_criteria.yellow: A 1:1 cadence exists, but rescheduling or gaps are still common.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers maintain a dependable 1:1 cadence that employees can count on.

controls.name: Structured 1:1 Agenda
controls.description: 1:1s use a shared agenda with contributions from both people, notes, and clear action items.
evidence_examples: Agenda template used across the team

scoring_criteria.red: 1:1s are unstructured and do not reliably produce follow-up.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some structure exists, but agendas, notes, or actions are inconsistently captured.
scoring_criteria.green: 1:1s are structured, bi-directional, and result in clear follow-up.

controls.name: Quarterly Goal Setting
controls.description: Managers and direct reports establish and track quarterly goals together.

scoring_criteria.red: Direct reports do not have clear or regularly reviewed quarterly goals.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Goals exist, but they are unevenly set or tracked.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers and employees set and review quarterly goals together.

controls.name: Bi-Directional Feedback Cadence
controls.description: Managers and direct reports exchange bi-directional feedback every 3 to 6 months.

scoring_criteria.red: Feedback is one-way, rare, or only happens when something is wrong.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Feedback cycles happen, but not with enough consistency or depth.
scoring_criteria.green: Feedback is regular, bi-directional, and expected as part of the manager relationship.

controls.name: Formal Performance Conversations
controls.description: Managers hold formal performance conversations every 3, 6, or 12 months based on the pace of organizational change.

scoring_criteria.red: Formal performance conversations are infrequent, unclear, or absent.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Performance conversations happen, but cadence or quality is inconsistent.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers hold formal performance conversations at a cadence appropriate to the business context.

controls.name: Performance on Results and Behaviors
controls.description: Performance conversations cover both what results were achieved and how they were achieved.

scoring_criteria.red: Performance is judged narrowly or informally, without a balanced view of outcomes and behaviors.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Managers discuss both dimensions sometimes, but not in a consistent framework.
scoring_criteria.green: Performance conversations clearly cover both outcomes and the behaviors behind them.

domains.name: Team Management
domains.description: Regular team operating rhythms that support alignment, decisions, and execution.

controls.name: Regular Team Meeting Cadence
controls.description: Managers hold weekly or biweekly team meetings with a dependable cadence.

scoring_criteria.red: Team meetings are irregular, often canceled, or low priority.
scoring_criteria.yellow: A cadence exists, but reschedules or inconsistency still reduce its value.
scoring_criteria.green: The team meeting cadence is dependable and protected.

controls.name: Meeting Reliability
controls.description: Managers arrive on time for meetings and avoid rescheduling except when necessary.

scoring_criteria.red: Managers routinely run late or reschedule, signaling that team time is not protected.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Meetings are usually reliable, but disruptions still happen too often.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers treat team time as a commitment and are consistently reliable.

controls.name: Effective Use of Team Time
controls.description: Team meetings are used to align on plans, make decisions, workshop difficult problems, and support execution.

scoring_criteria.red: Team meetings consume time without driving clarity, decisions, or support.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Meetings sometimes help the team, but value is inconsistent.
scoring_criteria.green: Team time is used deliberately for alignment, decisions, and unblocking work.

domains.name: Division and Organizational Clarity
domains.description: Foundational people systems that help employees understand roles, growth, and pay.

controls.name: Clear Job Descriptions
controls.description: All actively hired roles have clear job descriptions and an interview rubric.

scoring_criteria.red: Hiring roles are poorly defined and interview quality depends on individual managers.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Job descriptions and rubrics exist for some roles, but not consistently.
scoring_criteria.green: Every active role has a clear description and structured interview rubric.

controls.name: Job Ladders and Levels
controls.description: Job ladders and levels are defined and communicated clearly to employees.

scoring_criteria.red: Employees do not have a clear view of roles, expectations, or growth paths.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some ladder structure exists, but understanding is uneven.
scoring_criteria.green: Job ladders and levels are clear, documented, and understood by employees.

controls.name: Compensation Philosophy and Framework
controls.description: The organization has a compensation philosophy and a transparent compensation framework that employees can understand.

scoring_criteria.red: Compensation decisions appear opaque or inconsistent to employees.
scoring_criteria.yellow: A framework exists, but managers or employees cannot explain it clearly.
scoring_criteria.green: Compensation philosophy and framework are documented and understandable.

domains.name: Manager Operating Discipline
domains.description: Practices that help managers stay aligned, informed, and effective in their own role.

controls.name: Role and Team Clarity
controls.description: Managers understand their job description and their team's purpose and responsibilities.

scoring_criteria.red: Managers have weak clarity on their role or what their team is accountable for.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Role and team purpose are mostly clear, but important gaps remain.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers can clearly explain their role, team purpose, and boundaries.

controls.name: Quarterly Goal Alignment
controls.description: Managers have quarterly goals aligned with their own manager.

scoring_criteria.red: Managers operate without clear aligned quarterly goals.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Goals exist, but alignment or follow-through is inconsistent.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers have aligned quarterly goals and review progress with their manager.

controls.name: Regular 1:1s with Own Manager
controls.description: Managers hold regular 1:1s with their own manager.

scoring_criteria.red: Managers do not have a reliable support and alignment cadence with their own manager.
scoring_criteria.yellow: 1:1s exist, but they are inconsistent or low-value.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers have a dependable 1:1 cadence with their own manager.

controls.name: Access to Company Context
controls.description: Managers have ongoing access to context about company priorities and goals from leaders and peer managers.

scoring_criteria.red: Managers lack enough context to connect team decisions to company priorities.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some context is available, but not at a useful or reliable cadence.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers regularly receive and use company-level context in their decisions.

domains.name: Founder Mindset and Culture
domains.description: Management behaviors that create ownership, mission, trust, and broad empowerment.

controls.name: Founder Mindset
controls.description: Managers act like owners by treating company problems as their problems to solve, not someone else's.
evidence_examples: Examples of manager-led problem solving beyond role boundaries

scoring_criteria.red: Managers behave narrowly and escalate too quickly instead of taking ownership.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some managers show strong ownership, but it is not a clear expectation.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers consistently act with company-level ownership and initiative.

controls.name: Mission and Meaning
controls.description: Managers connect work to a mission that matters rather than treating execution as task administration.
evidence_examples: Team charter tied to company mission

scoring_criteria.red: Work is managed as disconnected tasks with little sense of purpose.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Mission is referenced, but not consistently tied to team priorities.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers consistently connect team work to a mission that matters.

controls.name: Trust Freedom and Authority
controls.description: Managers give people meaningful trust, freedom, and decision authority rather than controlling every detail.

scoring_criteria.red: Managers over-control work and keep too much authority to themselves.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some trust is given, but real authority remains narrow.
scoring_criteria.green: People have meaningful autonomy and decision authority, with managers providing support rather than control.

controls.name: Low-Status Environment
controls.description: Managers reduce status signals and create an environment where ideas can come from anywhere.
evidence_examples: Manager behavior examples that remove status barriers
Employee survey results on voice and inclusion

scoring_criteria.red: Status and hierarchy suppress dissent and good ideas.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Leaders want a low-status culture, but behavior is inconsistent.
scoring_criteria.green: Status barriers are actively reduced and people can contribute regardless of level.

domains.name: Hiring Excellence
domains.description: Practical recruiting and selection disciplines that raise talent quality instead of filling seats quickly.

controls.name: Recruiting Investment
controls.description: Leadership treats recruiting as a high-leverage investment and funds it before lower-priority HR spend.
evidence_examples: Leadership review of critical hiring roles

scoring_criteria.red: Recruiting is under-resourced and treated as an administrative task.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Recruiting receives some focused investment, but not enough for critical hiring goals.
scoring_criteria.green: Recruiting is treated as a core strategic investment.

controls.name: High Hiring Bar
controls.description: The company hires slowly enough to maintain a clear quality bar and aims to hire people stronger than the current team in meaningful ways.
evidence_examples: Offer decline reasons due to quality bar
Interview debrief notes on strengths added to the team

scoring_criteria.red: Hiring decisions prioritize speed or convenience over talent quality.
scoring_criteria.yellow: The company has a stated bar, but it bends too often under pressure.
scoring_criteria.green: Hiring consistently favors exceptional talent over short-term convenience.

controls.name: Objective Candidate Assessment
controls.description: Candidates are assessed through structured evidence rather than manager preference or intuition alone.

scoring_criteria.red: Candidate decisions are mostly subjective and weakly structured.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Structured assessment exists, but it is not applied consistently.
scoring_criteria.green: Candidate assessment is structured, evidence-based, and reasonably objective.

controls.name: Exceptional Candidate Sourcing
controls.description: Recruiting uses specific referrals, shared team sourcing effort, and creative outreach to find exceptional candidates.

scoring_criteria.red: Sourcing depends on generic inbound flow or passive recruiting alone.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some targeted sourcing exists, but effort is inconsistent.
scoring_criteria.green: The company actively uses specific referrals, shared sourcing, and creative outreach to find exceptional people.

controls.name: Compelling Candidate Close
controls.description: Candidates are given a clear reason to join through mission, role clarity, talent density, and real opportunity.

scoring_criteria.red: Candidates are expected to join without a thoughtful and differentiated close.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some teams close well, but the message and experience are inconsistent.
scoring_criteria.green: Candidates receive a clear compelling reason to join.

domains.name: Performance and Talent Management
domains.description: Performance systems that combine high standards, fairness, development, and differentiated support.

controls.name: Goal Quality
controls.description: Performance goals are set clearly enough that people know what success looks like and can be evaluated fairly.

scoring_criteria.red: Goals are vague, inconsistent, or not useful for evaluation and development.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some goals are clear, but goal quality varies too much.
scoring_criteria.green: Goals are clear, specific enough to assess, and aligned to team priorities.

controls.name: Peer Feedback and Calibration
controls.description: Managers gather peer feedback and use calibration to improve consistency in ratings and decisions.

scoring_criteria.red: Performance decisions are made without enough outside evidence or calibration.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Peer feedback and calibration happen, but not consistently enough.
scoring_criteria.green: Peer feedback and calibration are standard parts of performance decision-making.

controls.name: Separate Rewards from Development
controls.description: Managers clearly separate compensation or rating discussions from development conversations.

scoring_criteria.red: Development conversations are dominated or distorted by rewards discussions.
scoring_criteria.yellow: The distinction is known, but not consistently practiced.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers separate development coaching from rewards and rating conversations.

controls.name: Managing the Two Tails
controls.description: Managers actively support struggling employees while also paying close attention to top performers and their growth.
evidence_examples: Retention and growth plans for top performers

scoring_criteria.red: Managers ignore either struggling employees or top performers.
scoring_criteria.yellow: The team addresses one tail better than the other.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers intentionally support both struggling employees and top performers.

controls.name: Survey and Checklist Use
controls.description: Managers use surveys and checklists to test assumptions, find the truth, and nudge improvement.

scoring_criteria.red: Managers rely mostly on instinct and anecdote to assess team health and performance.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some survey or checklist use exists, but it is not a standard practice.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers use lightweight surveys and checklists to uncover truth and drive improvement.

controls.name: Manager Feedback Example
controls.description: Managers set a personal example by sharing feedback they receive and acting visibly on it.

scoring_criteria.red: Managers ask for feedback from others but do not model receiving it themselves.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Managers are somewhat open to feedback, but follow-through is not visible.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers visibly seek, share, and act on feedback as a role-model behavior.

domains.name: Learning and Institution Building
domains.description: Management practices that turn skill growth into a repeatable organizational capability.

controls.name: Deliberate Practice
controls.description: Skill building is broken into small repeatable lessons with clear feedback loops.

scoring_criteria.red: Learning is mostly passive and disconnected from repeated practice.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some deliberate practice exists, but it is not common or well-designed.
scoring_criteria.green: Learning includes repeated practice with clear feedback.

controls.name: Best People Teach
controls.description: The organization uses its strongest people to teach others and scale know-how.

scoring_criteria.red: Top performers are not meaningfully used to teach others.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some internal teaching happens, but not in a systematic way.
scoring_criteria.green: Strong people actively teach and raise the capability of others.

controls.name: Behavior-Change Focused Training
controls.description: Managers invest in courses and training only when there is evidence they change behavior on the job.

scoring_criteria.red: Training spend is weakly tied to actual behavior change or capability gains.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some learning investments are evaluated for impact, but not consistently.
scoring_criteria.green: Training investments are chosen and reviewed based on actual behavior change.

domains.name: Rewards and Support Systems
domains.description: Compensation and support practices that reinforce performance, fairness, and care without flattening outcomes.

controls.name: Differentiated Pay
controls.description: Compensation can vary meaningfully to reflect differences in contribution and sustained performance.

scoring_criteria.red: Compensation is too compressed to reflect meaningful performance differences.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some differentiation exists, but managers avoid using it fully.
scoring_criteria.green: Compensation meaningfully differentiates sustained contribution and impact.

controls.name: Celebrate Accomplishment Not Compensation
controls.description: Managers celebrate accomplishment and impact without making compensation the center of recognition.

scoring_criteria.red: Recognition is weak or overly tied to pay outcomes alone.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Accomplishment is recognized, but the practice is inconsistent.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers visibly celebrate accomplishment, learning, and impact.

controls.name: Thoughtful Failure Reward
controls.description: Managers recognize thoughtful failure and learning rather than punishing every unsuccessful attempt.

scoring_criteria.red: Thoughtful failure is treated the same as negligence or poor judgment.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Leaders say failure is acceptable, but incentives still discourage intelligent risk-taking.
scoring_criteria.green: Thoughtful failure is handled in a way that reinforces learning and good judgment.

controls.name: Employee Support and Friction Reduction
controls.description: Managers make work easier where they can, remove unnecessary friction, and show up when life hits employees hard.

scoring_criteria.red: Managers add friction and are absent when employees face significant personal difficulty.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Support is available, but not consistently and proactively.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers reduce friction and reliably support employees in hard moments.

domains.name: Nudging and Practical Change
domains.description: Managers use small experiments and thoughtful nudges rather than forcing behavior through blunt mandates alone.

controls.name: Reality-Based Management
controls.description: Managers distinguish between current reality and desired future state when designing change.

scoring_criteria.red: Managers design change based on what they wish were true, not on current reality.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Reality is considered, but change design still overestimates readiness.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers design change based on the actual current state and likely adoption path.

controls.name: Small Experiments
controls.description: Managers run many small experiments to improve culture, process, and employee outcomes.

scoring_criteria.red: Improvement efforts rely mostly on large one-shot changes with little testing.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some experiments occur, but not as a consistent habit.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers use small experiments regularly to learn and improve.

controls.name: Nudge Over Shove
controls.description: Managers shape behavior through smart defaults, examples, and lightweight incentives before using heavy-handed control.

scoring_criteria.red: Managers rely too quickly on mandates and pressure to drive change.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some nudging techniques are used, but not intentionally enough.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers use nudges, defaults, and examples to move behavior effectively.

domains.name: Mistakes, Transparency, and Repair
domains.description: How managers respond when they or the organization get something wrong.

controls.name: Admit Mistakes Transparently
controls.description: Managers admit mistakes, are transparent about them, and avoid defensive behavior.

scoring_criteria.red: Managers hide mistakes, spin them, or become defensive.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Managers admit mistakes sometimes, but transparency varies with situation.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers acknowledge mistakes quickly and transparently.

controls.name: Take Counsel Broadly
controls.description: Managers seek input from multiple directions before and after difficult decisions or mistakes.

scoring_criteria.red: Managers stay in a narrow echo chamber when problems arise.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Managers seek some input, but from too few perspectives.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers seek counsel from multiple directions before and after important decisions.

controls.name: Fix What Broke
controls.description: Managers follow mistakes through to concrete repair rather than stopping at apology or analysis.

scoring_criteria.red: Mistakes are acknowledged but not repaired thoroughly.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some corrective work happens, but follow-through is uneven.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers drive concrete repair actions until the underlying problem is addressed.

controls.name: Teach the Lesson
controls.description: Managers extract and share the lesson from mistakes so the organization improves rather than simply moving on.
evidence_examples: Process or policy update based on the lesson

scoring_criteria.red: Mistakes are not turned into shared learning.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some lessons are captured, but not consistently shared or applied.
scoring_criteria.green: Managers turn mistakes into teachable lessons that improve the wider organization.
